content.text: Researcher coordination is the structured collaboration process between an organization's security team and an external security researcher after a vulnerability has been reported and initially triaged. It covers the communication protocols, legal agreements, technical collaboration mechanisms, and decision-making processes that bridge the gap between the researcher's initial discovery and the public disclosure of the vulnerability and its fix.

For AI-specific vulnerabilities, coordination is more complex than for traditional software bugs. An AI vulnerability such as a reliable jailbreak or a prompt injection enabling agent privilege escalation may require collaboration with multiple internal teams: the AI safety team to understand the model-layer impact, the platform engineering team to remediate the serving infrastructure, and the product team to assess downstream effect on users. The coordination process must account for this cross-functional complexity.

The researcher coordination process determines whether a VDP operates as designed or breaks down into frustration and unilateral disclosure. Researchers who receive poor coordination — slow responses, unclear timelines, or lack of technical engagement — have little incentive to wait for the coordinated window to expire before publishing. Poor coordination is one of the most common causes of premature or contested disclosure.

Good coordination also produces better outcomes for users. A researcher who is genuinely engaged in the remediation process may identify additional attack vectors, help validate that the proposed fix actually addresses the root cause, or identify related issues that the internal team would have missed. Treating the researcher as a collaborator rather than a reporter improves both the quality of the fix and the researcher's incentive to work with the organization again in the future.
Reduces the risk of premature public disclosure by keeping researchers informed and engaged throughout the remediation window
Improves fix quality by giving researchers the opportunity to validate that the proposed patch addresses the underlying issue
Builds long-term relationships with high-quality researchers who become repeat contributors
Provides the researcher with the information they need to write an accurate technical writeup at disclosure time, reducing the risk of an incomplete or misleading public account

Creates a documented record of the coordination process that supports the organization's legal defense if the researcher later claims the fix was inadequate or the disclosure timeline was unreasonable

Sharing technical details of the vulnerability with the researcher to enable collaboration requires trusting that they will maintain confidentiality during the window; an NDA provides legal protection but adds friction
Weekly status updates and collaborative fix validation are valuable but time-intensive; the investment scales with report volume
When a fix is not ready at the end of the 90-day window, negotiating an extension requires the researcher's agreement; some researchers will decline and disclose unilaterally
AI team involvement vs. information containment:
Bringing in AI engineering or AI safety experts to collaborate on a vulnerability fix widens the circle of awareness of the issue before it is patched
The coordination process runs from initial acknowledgment through public disclosure, typically spanning 30 to 90 days. Each phase has defined responsibilities and communication norms.

Within 24 hours of receiving a report, the VDP triage contact sends the researcher a receipt acknowledgment. The acknowledgment confirms that the report was received, gives a case number or tracking reference, states the initial triage timeline (typically 5-10 business days), and provides a named point of contact with a direct communication channel for follow-up questions.

For significant vulnerabilities, particularly those involving model-layer issues or agentic system compromise, offer the researcher an optional mutual non-disclosure agreement. The NDA covers the period between initial triage and public disclosure and protects both parties: the organization's remediation details remain confidential, and the researcher's personal information and internal communications are not disclosed without consent. Many experienced security researchers prefer not to sign NDAs as a matter of principle; the offer should be optional, not required.

Once the vulnerability is validated, open a secure collaboration channel with the researcher. For AI-specific vulnerabilities, this typically involves providing access to a staging environment where the researcher can test the reproduction case without impacting production users, and connecting them with the AI engineering team members who are working on the fix. The researcher should be able to ask technical questions and receive substantive answers about the root cause and the proposed remediation approach.

Provide weekly status updates to the researcher covering the current fix status, any blockers or delays, and the anticipated deployment date. If the fix will not be ready within the original 90-day window, contact the researcher before the deadline to negotiate an extension. Provide specific justification for why additional time is needed rather than a generic request. Offer additional recognition or reward if an extension is requested.

Before the coordinated disclosure date, share a draft of the security advisory with the researcher for accuracy review. Give the researcher an opportunity to review the credit attribution, the technical description of the vulnerability, and the description of the fix. If the researcher plans to publish a technical writeup, coordinate the publication date so it does not precede the advisory.

attrs.alt: Researcher Coordination Process for AI Vulnerabilities

content.text: The swim-lane diagram shows the five-phase coordination timeline with responsibilities mapped across Researcher, Security, Legal, Engineering, and Product. The right side shows the key activities and timelines for each phase.
For every report that passes initial triage as in-scope and valid, regardless of severity
Immediately after a report is classified as critical or high severity — do not wait for full validation before opening the coordination channel
When a report touches a novel AI vulnerability class that requires specialist AI engineering involvement to understand and remediate
When the remediation window is at risk of being exceeded and extension negotiation is needed
When a researcher signals frustration or threatens to disclose without coordination

Document the minimum communication cadence for each severity level. For critical vulnerabilities: daily updates until the fix is deployed. For high: every three business days. For medium: weekly. Assign a named coordinator for each report so the researcher has a consistent contact.

Do not coordinate AI vulnerability details over standard email or ticketing systems that are not end-to-end encrypted. Use a secure channel such as a Signal thread, a private Slack workspace, or a managed VDP platform's encrypted messaging feature. For the staging environment, use a sandboxed AI deployment that mirrors production without containing real user data.

AI security engineers who triage and coordinate AI vulnerability reports need to understand both the technical vulnerability classes (prompt injection, model inversion, jailbreaks) and the coordination norms of the security research community. They should be familiar with the history of coordinated disclosure disputes and know how to de-escalate situations where a researcher is threatening early disclosure.

Maintain a running log of every communication with the researcher, every internal decision about the remediation timeline, and every extension negotiation. This documentation protects the organization if the researcher later disputes the coordination process or if regulatory authorities examine the incident response.

## Researcher Coordination — Communication Templates

### Template 1: Initial Acknowledgment (Day 0)

Subject: [ACME Security] Report Received — Case #2025-AI-042

Thank you for submitting this report to ACME's Vulnerability Disclosure Program.
We have received your report and assigned it case number 2025-AI-042.

Your point of contact is: Alex Chen, AI Security Engineer
Secure channel: [Signal / encrypted Slack link]

We will complete initial triage within 10 business days and provide you
with our assessment of scope, validity, and initial severity classification.

Please do not disclose this report publicly until we have completed
coordinated disclosure, which will occur within 90 days of this acknowledgment
unless we negotiate an extension with your agreement.

---

### Template 2: Triage Complete (Day 10)

Case #2025-AI-042 — Triage Complete

Scope: In scope (AI agent pipeline at agent.acme.com)
Validation: Reproduced successfully on staging environment
Severity: High (CVSS 8.2 + AI modifier for agentic action enabled)
Vulnerability class: Indirect prompt injection via retrieved web content

Proposed remediation:
  - Input sanitization on retrieved content before agent context injection
  - Output monitoring for anomalous tool-call patterns
  
Estimated fix deployment: 45 days from today
Coordinated disclosure target: [Date]

We would like to offer you access to our AI staging environment to review
the reproduction case. Are you available for a 30-minute call this week?

---

### Template 3: Extension Request (Day 85)

Case #2025-AI-042 — Extension Request

Our current fix deployment is delayed by 15 days due to a dependency
on an upstream library update that is not yet released (openai-python v2.1.0).

We are requesting a 21-day extension to the coordinated disclosure window,
moving the target disclosure date from [original date] to [new date].

In recognition of your patience, we are increasing your bounty award
from $5,000 to $7,500 upon disclosure.

Please confirm whether you agree to this extension by [date].

A researcher reported an indirect prompt injection in a commercial AI assistant product that caused it to exfiltrate user conversation history when the user's browser visited a maliciously crafted web page. The vendor's security team acknowledged within 6 hours and opened a Signal thread with the researcher within 24 hours.

Over the following 30 days, the vendor shared a private staging environment where the researcher tested the fix candidate, identified that the initial patch was incomplete (it blocked one exfiltration vector but not a second via a different tool call path), and worked with the engineering team to extend the fix to cover both vectors. The coordinated disclosure went out at day 60 with a joint advisory, a CVE assigned by the vendor as a CNA, a $20,000 bounty, and a detailed technical writeup co-authored with the researcher. The researcher cited the coordination experience as a reason to report future issues to the same vendor first.
Treating the researcher as a threat rather than a collaborator:
Adversarial language or legal threats in coordination communications damage the relationship and increase the likelihood of premature disclosure
Going silent for more than a week without explanation signals that the organization is not taking the report seriously and prompts researchers to set unilateral disclosure deadlines
Requiring an NDA as a condition of receiving any coordination information is a common source of researcher friction; experienced researchers often refuse NDAs as a matter of principle
Not involving AI specialists in technical collaboration:
AI vulnerability coordination that is handled entirely by a traditional security team without AI engineering involvement often produces fixes that are technically incorrect or incomplete
Failing to credit the researcher in the public advisory after they contributed to the fix is a reputational harm that discourages future reports
Assign a named coordinator to every valid report and ensure they have authority to make decisions without escalating every question
Maintain weekly status updates at minimum; daily for critical vulnerabilities until the fix is deployed
Provide access to a staging environment that mirrors production without real user data for AI vulnerability reproduction
Involve AI engineering and AI safety specialists in technical collaboration for model-layer vulnerabilities
Make the NDA optional, not required; treat it as a mutual protection mechanism, not a gatekeeping measure
Credit the researcher in the public advisory and in your Hall of Fame regardless of whether they received a monetary reward
Document every coordination decision and communication in case the process is later disputed

Coordination quality tends to degrade as report volume scales unless the process is deliberately structured. At low volume, ad-hoc coordination is manageable. At high volume, the coordination team needs a ticketing system that tracks SLA compliance per report, automated reminders when a status update is due, and templates for common communication scenarios so coordinators are not writing from scratch each time.

For organizations running public bug bounty programs with high report volumes, consider a tiered coordination model where a junior security analyst handles initial triage and routine coordination for medium and low severity reports, freeing senior AI security engineers for the deeper technical collaboration required by critical and high severity reports.
HackerOne and Bugcrowd provide built-in coordination workflows, SLA tracking, messaging, and payment — reducing the coordination overhead of managing reports via email
Signal, Keybase, or a private encrypted Slack workspace for sensitive technical discussions that should not travel over standard email
Isolated cloud deployments (AWS, Azure, GCP) that mirror the production AI system without real user data — provide researcher access via short-lived credentials
Register as a CVE Numbering Authority or work through an existing CNA such as HackerOne to assign CVE IDs for accepted vulnerabilities

The coordination channel itself is a security surface. Internal details of an unpatched AI vulnerability shared with a researcher via an unencrypted channel could be intercepted by a third party who then exploits the vulnerability before the fix is deployed. Use encrypted channels for all coordination communications and limit the distribution of vulnerability details to the minimum set of people who need them to execute the fix.

Legal documents shared during coordination, including NDAs and any written acknowledgment of the vulnerability's existence, should be reviewed by Legal before being shared, as they may have implications for the organization's position in subsequent litigation or regulatory proceedings.
Researcher coordination is the human process that determines whether a VDP succeeds or breaks down into disputed disclosure
Assign a named coordinator with decision authority to every valid report and maintain weekly status updates at minimum
Provide access to a sandboxed staging environment for AI vulnerability reproduction so researchers can validate fixes without impacting production
Involve AI engineering and AI safety specialists in technical collaboration for model-layer vulnerabilities, not just the traditional security team
Make NDAs optional rather than required; adversarial coordination language increases disclosure risk
Credit researchers in the public advisory regardless of monetary reward; omitting credit is a reputational harm that discourages future reports
Document every coordination decision and communication to protect the organization if the process is later disputed
